question_title: How many candidates will file nominations for the Meghalaya Rajya Sabha seat by the June 8, 2026 deadline?
summary: The most likely outcome for Meghalaya's Rajya Sabha nomination count is 1 candidate (James PK Sangma, NPP/MDA-II), with P(1)≈77%. The critical structural constraint is that Meghalaya's opposition has only 9 MLAs (AITC 5 + VPP 4) after INC members defected to NPP, which is below the 10-proposer threshold required for filing RS nominations. The MDA-II officially announced their candidate on June 3, 2026, with filing expected June 5. No opposition candidate has been announced with only 5 days until the June 8 deadline. Historical base rates for single-seat small state RS elections show ~90-95% result in exactly 1 candidate. The ~22.5% probability for 2+ candidates accounts for residual uncertainty about exact INC MLA status, the possibility of creative coalition-building (e.g., 9 opposition + 1 sympathetic independent), potential dissident filings, and the 2020 Meghalaya precedent of 2 candidates (though conditions then were materially different with Congress having ~17 MLAs).

factors.description: MDA-II has officially announced James PK Sangma as candidate on June 3, filing expected June 5 - guarantees at least 1 nomination

Opposition has only 9 MLAs (AITC 5 + VPP 4) - below the 10-proposer threshold. INC MLAs have defected to NPP (confirmed via Wikipedia and Feb 2026 news). Structurally very difficult for opposition to file.
Historical base rate: ~90-95% of single-seat small state RS elections have exactly 1 candidate (2020-2024 data across ~20 elections)
No opposition party has announced a candidate as of June 3, with deadline June 8 - very little time remaining
2020 Meghalaya precedent had 2 candidates (Kharlukhi vs Khyriem), but Congress had ~17 MLAs then vs ~0 now
Two independent MLAs exist who could theoretically help opposition reach 10 proposers, but they support the government
Residual possibilities: dissident within MDA, creative coalition building, nuisance filing with invalid proposers that still counts

reasoning:
## Forecast Summary

**Question**: How many candidates will file nominations for the Meghalaya Rajya Sabha seat by the June 8, 2026 deadline?

**Distribution**: Heavily concentrated at 1, with a tail extending to 2-4.

| Outcome | Probability |
|---------|------------|
| 0 | 0.5% |
| 1 | 77% |
| 2 | 17% |
| 3 | 4% |
| 4+ | 1.5% |

**Expected value**: ~1.3 candidates

**Key reasoning**:
1. **MDA-II confirmed James PK Sangma** as their candidate on June 3 (filing expected June 5) — at least 1 filing is virtually certain
2. **Critical structural barrier**: Opposition has only 9 MLAs (AITC 5 + VPP 4), which is *below* the 10-MLA proposer threshold required to file a nomination. INC's 2023 MLAs have defected to NPP (confirmed for Celestine Lyngdoh and Charles Marngar)
3. **No opposition candidate announced** with just 5 days until the deadline
4. **Historical base rate**: ~90-95% of single-seat small state RS elections result in exactly 1 candidate
5. **Residual probability (22.5%) for 2+** accounts for: creative coalition building (9 opposition + 1 independent), potential dissidents, or uncertainty about exact INC MLA count
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) How many candidates will file nominations for the Meghalaya Rajya Sabha seat by the June 8, 2026 deadline?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43766)

[Meghalaya Rajya Sabha Election Results 2020: NPP’s Kharlukhi Wins Lone RS Seat](https://www.india.com/news/india/meghalaya-rajya-sabha-election-results-2020-npps-kharlukhi-wins-lone-rs-seat-4062899/)
[(Metaculus) Will the June 2026 Meghalaya Rajya Sabha election be contested (i.e., have more than one candidate remaining after the withdrawal deadline)?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43757)
resolution_criteria:
This question resolves as the total number of candidates who file at least one nomination paper for the Meghalaya Rajya Sabha seat by the close of nominations on 8 June 2026, as reported by the Returning Officer / Chief Electoral Officer, Meghalaya (https://ceomeghalaya.nic.in/) or the Election Commission of India. The count is of distinct individuals who filed nominations, regardless of whether the nomination is later accepted at scrutiny (9 June) or withdrawn (by 11 June). For example, if three distinct people filed nominations — even if one is later rejected and one withdraws — the answer is 3.

If the CEO Meghalaya page does not display the figure directly, the count from the Wikipedia article "2026 Rajya Sabha elections" (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Rajya_Sabha_elections) Meghalaya section, or reporting in [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) such as The Shillong Times, may be used. If no authoritative source reports a number by 14 June 2026, the question resolves based on the best-available reporting on the list of nominees published after scrutiny on 9 June 2026.
fine_print: Only nominations filed for the lone Meghalaya Rajya Sabha seat with term commencing in/around June 2026 count. Multiple nomination papers (sets) filed by the same individual count as one candidate (Rajya Sabha rules permit a candidate to file up to four sets of nomination papers). Nominations filed but withdrawn before the close of nominations on 8 June 2026 still count if officially received and recorded by the Returning Officer.

factors.description: MDA-II has officially announced James PK Sangma as candidate on June 3, filing expected June 5 - guarantees at least 1 nomination
Opposition has only 9 MLAs (AITC 5 + VPP 4) which is below the 10-proposer threshold required for filing nominations - structurally impossible for opposition to file without help from ruling side
Historical base rate: ~18-19 out of ~20 single-seat small state RS elections in 2020-2024 had exactly 1 candidate
In 2020, Meghalaya RS election had 2 candidates (Kharlukhi vs Khyriem), but Congress had ~17 MLAs then vs 0 now
No opposition party has announced a candidate as of June 3, with deadline June 8 - very little time remaining
Small residual possibility of dissident within MDA filing, independent with ruling-side support, or invalid nomination filing that still counts

input.anchor: Historical base rate for single-seat small state RS elections: ~90-95% have exactly 1 candidate (from 2020-2024 data across ~20 elections). Current Meghalaya case is even more constrained because opposition has only 9 MLAs, below the 10-proposer requirement.
input.assessment: The dominant outcome is 1 candidate (James PK Sangma, NPP/MDA-II). The critical structural factor is that Meghalaya's opposition has only 9 MLAs total, which is below the 10-proposer threshold required for filing RS nominations. This makes it structurally impossible for the opposition to field a candidate without cooperation from ruling-side MLAs. Combined with no opposition candidate announcement as of June 3 and only 5 days until the June 8 deadline, the probability of exactly 1 candidate is ~78%. There's a ~15.5% chance of 2 candidates (via creative coalition building, a dissident, or a technically invalid filing), and ~6.5% for 3+. The 2020 Meghalaya precedent (2 candidates) is not directly applicable because Congress then had 17+ MLAs, enough to propose their own candidate.
input.calibration_notes: My estimate is consistent with the historical base rate (~90-95% single candidate) but I've adjusted slightly upward for 1 candidate given the additional structural constraint of the 10-proposer threshold being unachievable by the current opposition. The ~78% for 1 candidate is actually somewhat lower than the base rate would suggest, reflecting some residual uncertainty about potential dissidents or creative nomination strategies.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Whether any INC MLAs still exist in Meghalaya (conflicting data between sources). 2. Whether any ruling coalition MLA would defect to support an opposition nomination. 3. Whether any independent or nuisance candidate might file with invalid proposers. 4. Exact party composition of the 60-member assembly as of June 2026.
input.tool_audit: Research tool: Very useful for Meghalaya 2026 candidate info (James Sangma confirmed), assembly composition, and election timeline. Historical RS data from training knowledge had some inaccuracies (listed 2020 Meghalaya as unopposed when it was actually contested). Web search: Confirmed the 2020 contest (Kharlukhi vs Khyriem). CEO Meghalaya website timed out. Wikipedia 2026 RS page had limited detail on Meghalaya specifically.
input.process_reflection: The research tool was effective for getting the current political landscape but its historical data on small-state RS elections was drawn from training knowledge and had errors (claiming 2020 Meghalaya was unopposed). Direct web search was needed to verify the 2020 contest. The 10-proposer constraint is the key insight that wasn't immediately obvious from the question text - it fundamentally constrains the outcome distribution.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Review Summary

I found a critical cross-question consistency error and a potentially material factual issue.

### 1. Cross-Question Consistency Failure (FAIL)

The agent forecasted a closely related binary question (Q43757) just one day earlier: "Will the June 2026 Meghalaya Rajya Sabha election be contested (i.e., have more than one candidate remaining after the withdrawal deadline)?" — probability: **35% YES**.

The current discrete forecast (Q43766) gives P(2+ candidates file nominations) = 1 - P(X≤1) = 1 - 0.785 = **21.5%**.

This is **logically inconsistent**. Filing nominations is a strict prerequisite for remaining after withdrawal. Therefore P(2+ file) ≥ P(2+ remain after withdrawal) must hold. But 21.5% < 35% violates this constraint. The discrete forecast assigns too little probability to 2+ candidates.

To be consistent with the binary forecast, P(2+ file) should be **at least 35%**, which means P(X=1) should be at most ~65%, not 78%. The center of 1.0 may still be correct, but the distribution should have more mass at 2+.

My independent estimate based on the evidence: P(X=1) ≈ 62-65%, P(X=2) ≈ 25-28%, P(X≥3) ≈ 7-10%. This gives a center around 1.0 with range 1.0-2.0, but with a meaningfully different distribution shape.

### 2. Assembly Composition Uncertainty — Possible Factual Issue (Contributes to fail)

**Factor 2** claims: "Opposition has only 9 MLAs (AITC 5 + VPP 4) which is below the 10-proposer threshold required for filing nominations — structurally impossible for opposition to file without help from ruling side" (logit=+1.5, confidence=0.9).

This is the second-strongest factor after Factor 1 and forms the structural backbone of the forecast. However, it rests on uncertain assembly composition data:

- The current session's research cites Wikipedia "Meghalaya Legislative Assembly" showing current composition: NPP 33, UDP 12, AITC 5, VPP 4, BJP 2, HSPDP 2, Independents 2 — **no INC**. (Total: 60)
- The prior session (Q43757, June 2) verified via bankbazaar.com that INC has **5 MLAs** currently: Charles Marngar, Celestine Lyngdoh, Gabriel Wahlang, Ronnie V. Lyngdoh, Saleng A. Sangma.
- The worldview research entry (`meghalaya-legislative-assembly-party-composition-2-63796e6c.json`) lists INC with 5 seats based on 2023 election data, but notes it could not verify 2025-2026 developments.

The Wikipedia data (NPP 33 vs 26 at 2023 election) implies significant defections occurred. Whether INC's 5 MLAs formally defected is unclear — the bankbazaar data from just one day earlier shows them still listed under INC. If INC retains even 5 MLAs, the opposition total is 14 (INC 5 + AITC 5 + VPP 4), which is **above** the 10-proposer threshold, and the "structural impossibility" argument collapses entirely.

The agent itself acknowledged this uncertainty in its adversarial self-examination: "My assembly composition data may be incomplete or outdated... If INC retains even 1-2 MLAs, they could combine with AITC (5) and VPP (4) to reach the 10-proposer threshold and field a candidate." Despite recognizing this risk, the agent assigned Factor 2 a confidence of 0.9 and logit of +1.5, which seems too high given the documented conflicting evidence.

### 3. Factor Verification

**Factor 1 (James Sangma announced, logit=+3.0):** ✅ Verified. EastMojo and Times of India articles confirm the June 3 announcement. Guarantees at least 1 filing.

**Factor 2 (Opposition has 9 MLAs below threshold, logit=+1.5):** ⚠️ Core claim is in the trace (Wikipedia source), but contradicted by prior session's verified data showing INC with 5 MLAs. The "structurally impossible" claim is potentially wrong. See above.

**Factor 3 (Historical base rate ~90-95% have 1 candidate, logit=+1.0):** ⚠️ The historical data was generated from training knowledge, not live data. The research sub-agent listed the 2020 Meghalaya election as "Unopposed" when it was actually contested (2 candidates). The agent caught this error and adjusted, but the "~18-19 out of ~20" figure may still be imprecise. The correct interpretation appears to be that the 2020 Meghalaya election was the one exception, making it ~19/20 in the data they could verify.

**Factor 4 (2020 had 2 candidates, logit=+0.3):** ✅ Verified via EastMojo article: Kharlukhi defeated Khyriem 39-19. Factor correctly notes this precedent exists but is less applicable now.

**Factor 5 (No opposition candidate announced, logit=+0.8):** ✅ Verified. Multiple web searches found no opposition announcements.

**Factor 6 (Residual possibility, logit=-0.5):** ✅ Reasonable residual uncertainty factor.

### 4. Double-counting Check

Factors 2, 3, and 5 all push toward 1 candidate and partially overlap:
- Factor 2 (opposition below threshold) and Factor 3 (historical rate of 1 candidate) share the same underlying structural explanation — dominant ruling coalitions in small states.
- Factor 5 (no opposition announcement) is partially explained by Factor 2 (they can't meet the threshold).
Some mild double-counting, but given moderate logit values, the impact is ~5-10pp.

### 5. Resolution Criteria Alignment

✅ Resolution criteria are clearly specified and the factors engage with the correct metric (number of distinct individuals who file nominations, regardless of acceptance/withdrawal).

### 6. Counterargument Assessment

The counterargument is reasonably strong — it identifies the assembly composition uncertainty, the question author's phrasing suggesting token candidates are realistic, and the possibility of cross-party proposer cooperation. It estimates a smart disagreer at 30-40% for P(2+), which is actually consistent with the binary forecast but inconsistent with the discrete forecast's own 22%. This internal tension reinforces the conclusion that the 78% for exactly 1 candidate is too high.

### 7. Confidence Assessment

Agent confidence of 0.55 is appropriately moderate given the genuine uncertainties, but the distribution itself is too concentrated on 1 given the evidence.

### 8. Tool Audit

The agent says "CEO Meghalaya website timed out" — this matches the trace showing a timeout error on ceomeghalaya.nic.in (line 366). The 66.7% error rate on fetch_url (2 errors in 3 calls) is consistent with the trace showing one timeout and one 403 error.

### Verdict: FAIL

The primary reason is the cross-question consistency failure. The agent's own prior forecast (from one day earlier, same election, approved by the same review process) implies P(2+ file nominations) ≥ 35%, but the current forecast gives only 21.5%. This is a logical contradiction — you cannot have fewer filers than remainers. The correct P(X=1) should be closer to 62-65%, not 78%. The discrete forecast needs to shift ~13-16pp of mass from X=1 to X≥2 to be consistent with the prior binary forecast and the available evidence.

Wikipedia 2026 RS page had limited Meghalaya detail. Prior forecast data from Q43757 was critical for consistency check.

The INC defection confirmation from web search was important for understanding the true assembly composition. The main friction point was conflicting data sources on assembly composition.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Assessment

### What I Checked

I read the full research trace (735 lines), the prior Q43757 binary forecast, and relevant worldview research entries. I verified each factor's claims against the trace evidence and checked cross-question consistency.

### Factor Verification

**Factor 1 (James PK Sangma announced, logit=+3.0):** ✅ Verified. EastMojo and Times of India sources in trace confirm MDA-II announced James PK Sangma on June 3, 2026, with nomination filing expected June 5.

**Factor 2 (Opposition has only 9 MLAs, logit=+1.2):** ✅ Verified. The research tool returned an assembly composition table showing AITC 5 + VPP 4 = 9 opposition. Web searches confirmed Celestine Lyngdoh and Charles Marngar (won on INC tickets in 2023) now appear as NPP legislators on Wikipedia and in Feb 2026 news articles. Congress suspended them in Aug 2024 per KRC Times. The defection claim is well-grounded.

**Factor 3 (Historical base rate ~90-95%, logit=+1.0):** ⚠️ The historical research was based on training knowledge that incorrectly claimed the 2020 Meghalaya RS election was "Unopposed." The agent caught this error (trace line ~490-496) and self-corrected. However, the "90-95%" figure is built on this unreliable dataset. The corrected data (1 contested out of ~20 small-state elections) actually supports ~95%, so the claim is approximately right despite the flawed source.

**Factor 4 (No opposition announcement, logit=+0.8):** ✅ Verified. Multiple web searches for opposition candidates returned no results.

**Factor 5 (Cross-question consistency, logit=-1.0):** ✅ The Q43757 forecast of 35% is confirmed from the stored forecast file. The logical constraint P(2+ file) ≥ P(2+ remain after withdrawal) is correctly applied.

**Factor 6 (2020 precedent, logit=+0.3):** ✅ Verified. EastMojo article confirms "Dr Kharlukhi defeated Congress candidate, Kennedy Khyriem by 20 votes."

**Factor 7 (Residual possibilities, logit=-0.5):** Reasonable catch-all for uncertainty.

### No Hallucinated Evidence

All core factual claims are grounded in the research trace.

### No Wrong-Direction Factors

All logit signs match their descriptions.

### Resolution Criteria Alignment

✅ The question asks for distinct individuals who filed nominations. The factors correctly address the question of how many will file.

### Critical Error: Probability Assessment

**The agent's distribution (P(1)=62%, P(2+)=37.5%) is meaningfully wrong because it's anchored on a stale, incorrect prior forecast.**

The agent's Q43757 binary forecast (35% for contested) was made on June 2 based on "18 non-MDA MLAs total" (INC 5, TMC 5, VPP 4, Independents 4). The current session discovered that INC MLAs have defected to NPP, reducing actual opposition to just 9 MLAs — below the 10-proposer threshold needed to file a nomination. The agent acknowledges this in its adversarial self-examination: "The 35% binary forecast for 'contested' was based on an assessment that described '18 non-MDA MLAs' — suggesting a very different understanding of the assembly than the 9-MLA opposition I've identified."

Despite recognizing this, the agent forces consistency with the stale prior, setting P(2+ file) ≈ 37.5% to match the old 35% binary forecast. This is backwards — the new information (9 MLAs < 10-proposer threshold) should propagate to revise the understanding, not be constrained by a forecast built on now-falsified data.

**My independent estimate based on the evidence:**
- P(0) ≈ 0.5%
- P(1) ≈ 75-80%: Opposition structurally cannot meet 10-proposer threshold with only 9 MLAs; no announcement with 5 days remaining; MDA has overwhelming 51/60 majority; historical base rate strongly favors 1 candidate
- P(2) ≈ 15-18%: Possible via creative coalition (e.g., 9 opposition + 1 independent/sympathizer), dissident MDA filing, or someone filing with invalid proposers (which would still count per resolution criteria)
- P(3+) ≈ 3-5%

This gives P(2+) ≈ 20-23%, meaningfully lower than the agent's 37.5% — a ~15pp divergence. The agent is over-weighting cross-question consistency with a known-to-be-wrong prior and under-weighting the structural impossibility created by 9 opposition MLAs vs. 10-proposer threshold.

### Tool Audit Discrepancy

The agent's tool audit says "CEO Meghalaya website timed out" but doesn't mention the overall 66.7% fetch_url error rate (2 of 3 calls failed). This is a minor omission — the ground truth shows 3 fetch_url calls with 66.7% errors, not just a single CEO timeout.

input.assessment: Final distribution: P(0)=0.5%, P(1)=77%, P(2)=17%, P(3)=4%, P(4+)=1.5%. Expected value ~1.3. The dominant outcome is 1 candidate. The prior binary forecast of 35% was based on incorrect assembly composition data (18 non-MDA MLAs vs actual 9). Both forecasts should be updated based on current evidence showing INC defections to NPP. The 10-proposer threshold is the critical structural barrier - with only 9 opposition MLAs, the opposition literally cannot file without cooperation from at least one ruling-side MLA or independent. The ~22.5% probability for 2+ candidates accounts for creative coalition building, potential dissident filings, and residual uncertainty about exact assembly composition.

CEO Meghalaya website timed out. India.com returned 403. Prior forecast data (Q43757) useful for understanding context but had stale assembly data.
input.process_reflection: The premortem reviewer's first pass correctly identified the cross-question inconsistency. Its second pass then correctly identified that forcing consistency with a stale, wrong prior was itself an error. This two-pass feedback loop was valuable but highlighted a tension: cross-question consistency is important, but both forecasts should be updated when new evidence invalidates the basis of the prior forecast.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Checks Performed

### Resolution Criteria Recovery ✅
The agent fetched the Metaculus question details (post_id 43766) at the start of the session, obtaining full resolution criteria, fine print, and background info. Resolution criteria were properly engaged — the agent correctly focuses on "distinct individuals who filed nominations" regardless of scrutiny/withdrawal outcomes.

### Hallucinated Evidence Check ✅
All major factual claims are grounded in the trace:
- **Factor 1 (James Sangma announced as MDA-II candidate June 3)**: Confirmed by EastMojo ("Meghalaya: MDA-II backs James PK Sangma for Rajya Sabha polls") and Times of India sources in the research output.
- **Factor 2 (Opposition has 9 MLAs, below 10-proposer threshold)**: Confirmed by Wikipedia (Meghalaya Legislative Assembly) showing NPP 33, UDP 12, AITC 5, VPP 4, BJP 2, HSPDP 2, Ind 2. INC defections verified through web search: Celestine Lyngdoh now described as "NPP legislator" (hubnetwork.in, Feb 2026), Charles Marngar likewise now NPP (Wikipedia + Feb 2026 news), both suspended from Congress in Aug 2024.
- **Factor 3 (Historical base rate ~90-95%)**: Based on training data compilation showing 0 contested small-state elections in 2022 and 2020, 1 in 2024 (Himachal Pradesh). The agent correctly caught that the research sub-agent had incorrectly listed Meghalaya 2020 as "Unopposed" when it was actually contested.
- **Factor 5 (2020 Meghalaya had 2 candidates)**: Confirmed by EastMojo article stating "Dr Kharlukhi defeated Congress candidate, Kennedy Khyriem by 20 votes."

### Double-Counting Check ✅
No significant double-counting detected. Factors 2 (structural impossibility) and 4 (no announced opposition candidate) draw on different evidence types — one is structural analysis, the other is observed behavior. Factors 3 and 5 both relate to historical precedent but cover different aspects (general base rate vs. Meghalaya-specific precedent).

### Wrong-Direction Factors ✅
All logit signs are consistent with factor descriptions. Positive logits push toward 1 candidate (the modal outcome), negative logits push toward higher counts.

### Contradictory Assessment Check ✅
The assessment is consistent with the factors — it correctly identifies P(1)=77% as the dominant outcome driven by the 10-proposer structural barrier, with residual probability for creative coalition-building.

### Weak Counterargument Check ✅
The counterargument is genuinely strong. It constructs a coherent alternative story: (1) only 2 of 5 INC defections confirmed, leaving open 3 remaining INC MLAs that would push opposition to 12 (above threshold); (2) independents could sign nomination papers without political cost; (3) AITC might want symbolic candidacy post-West Bengal loss; (4) the question description itself mentions token/independent filings as realistic scenarios. This is not a token gesture.

### Cross-Question Consistency ⚠️ WARN
The agent's prior forecast for Q43757 ("Will the election be contested?") gave P(YES) = 35%, meaning P(2+ candidates remaining after withdrawal) = 35%. Logically, P(2+ filing nominations) must be ≥ P(2+ remaining after withdrawal). The current forecast gives P(2+ filing) = 22.5%, violating this constraint.

The agent explicitly grappled with this in the trace — initially adjusting to P(2+)=37.5% for consistency, then being told by the reflection reviewer that the prior 35% was based on erroneous assembly composition data ("18 non-MDA MLAs" vs the actual 9), and ultimately settling on 22.5%.

This is a genuine tension. The prior forecast's Factor 4 stated "Multiple opposition parties exist (INC 5, TMC 5, VPP 4, Independents 4 = 18 non-MDA MLAs)" — but the current session conclusively shows INC defected to NPP, leaving only 9 opposition MLAs. Both forecasts should be updated consistently. Since the agent cannot retroactively change Q43757, this creates an acknowledged inconsistency. The explanation is legitimate (new information about INC defections), but the inconsistency exists in the submitted forecast record. This is a **warn** rather than a fail because: (a) the agent identified the issue, (b) the explanation is well-grounded in new verified evidence, and (c) the current forecast would be wrong if forced to match the stale prior.

### Probability Assessment ✅
My independent estimate: P(1) ≈ 75-80%, P(2) ≈ 15-18%, P(3+) ≈ 3-5%. The agent's P(1)=77%, P(2)=17%, P(3)=4% sits squarely within my range. The key structural facts — 9 opposition MLAs below the 10-proposer threshold, no announced opposition candidate, strong historical precedent for unopposed elections in small states — all support 1 candidate as the overwhelming favorite. The ~22.5% tail for 2+ appropriately accounts for the uncertainty about remaining INC MLAs and creative coalition scenarios.

### Tool Audit Check ✅
The agent states "CEO Meghalaya website timed out" — confirmed in trace (ceomeghalaya.nic.in returned "Timeout"). "India.com returned 403" — confirmed. The agent doesn't overstate success. Ground truth shows 13.3% error rate (4/30 calls), with fetch_url at 66.7% (2/3) and premortem at 100% (2/2). The agent's audit is vague but not contradictory.

### Overconfident Self-Assessment Check ✅
Confidence of 0.50 is appropriate given: uncertainty about exact INC MLA count (only 2 of 5 defections confirmed), tension with prior forecast, and limited opposition news with 5 days still remaining before deadline.

### Anchor Divergence Check ✅
The anchor is "90-95% of single-seat small state RS elections have exactly 1 candidate." The final P(1)=77% is a departure of ~13-18pp, driven by acknowledged residual uncertainty about INC MLA status and the 2020 Meghalaya precedent. This departure is reasonable and well-explained.

### Worldview Research Contradiction Check
The worldview research entry `meghalaya-rajya-sabha-election-june-2026-candidate-a6704725.json` (from a prior session) shows INC at 5 seats based on 2023 election results, while the current session's research shows INC at 0 (defected to NPP). This is a stale entry (state: "fresh" but data_points as_of 2023-02-28) that the agent explicitly superseded with more recent evidence from the current session.
